text: Welcome to another podcast from School of Surgery. Today we're going to talk about anti-reflux surgery.
I'm Keaton Jones, Academic Clinical Fellow in General Surgery at the Oxford Deanery.

Today's learning outcomes include knowledge of the relevant surgical anatomy, key operative steps of the most frequently performed of the anti-reflux operations, the Nissen three hundred and sixty degree fundoplication, knowledge of alternative anti-reflux laparoscopic procedures, and recollection of the important complications of anti-reflux surgery.
Starting off with a schematic representation of the initial anatomy seen when a lap scope is passed into the abdomen facing the left hypochondrium.
The laparoscopic view shows the greater omentum, liver, and stomach, with minimal other structures visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon retracts the liver to reveal an initial intraoperative view, displaying the liver, lesser omentum, stomach, and greater omentum at the first stage of the fundoplication procedure.
The schematic representation displays the greater omentum, spleen, liver, stomach, anterior vagus nerve, and lesser omentum, along with the right and left crura of the diaphragm.
The video displays the greater curve, fundus, angle of His, lesser curve, and pylorus, highlighting key components of the gastric anatomy relevant to the fundoplication procedure.
So in terms of the operative steps,
First and foremost, we will talk about the patient positioning, which is extremely important for this procedure, and port insertion.

The surgeon retracts the liver to expose the surgical field, initiating peritoneal dissection. Short gastric vessels are divided, followed by esophageal and crural dissection. A posterior window is formed, and suturing is performed for crural repair, culminating in a 360-degree wrap around the esophagus.
The patient is positioned appropriately for the fundoplication procedure, followed by the insertion of surgical ports to facilitate access to the abdominal cavity.

The patient is essentially positioned into an armchair position. This requires a special operation table. Essentially, to start with, the knees are split and flexed. Subsequently, the hips are flexed to approximately ten degrees. The whole table is brought head up. The patient sits down in the table looking quite like they're in an armchair.
The surgeon stands between the patient's legs, facing directly towards the patient, ready to begin the surgical procedure.

The laparoscopic setup for a Nissen fundoplication includes a minimum of five ports, with two or three being ten millimeters in size. One port accommodates the camera, while another is used for a ten millimeter Babcock retractor.

The surgeon insufflates the abdomen using a varus needle at Palmer's point, located at the midpoint of the left subcostal margin. A 5mm port is then placed at the site of the initial needle insertion.

The surgeon places a camera port, measuring ten or twelve millimeters, a hand breadth below the xiphoid process, slightly left of the midline. An assistant port, typically ten millimeters, is positioned for a Babcock retractor. A liver retractor is inserted through an epigastric port, while two additional five millimeter ports are used by the surgeon for the procedure.
The surgeon uses a liver retractor inserted through the epigastric port to elevate the liver, providing better access to the surgical field for the fundoplication procedure.

The surgeon uses a Nathanson liver retractor to elevate the left hemiliver superiorly, allowing clear visualization of the stomach and surrounding structures necessary for the fundoplication procedure.
The surgeon inserts a five millimeter port in the epigastrium, which is then removed to guide the placement of the Nathanson liver retractor, resembling a shepherd's hook.

The Nathanson liver retractor is inserted hook side first into the abdomen, applying smooth and equal pressure underneath the liver to elevate it. The remaining arm of the retractor is fixed onto a table fixation device.

The surgeon inserts the five millimeter epigastric port, followed by the insertion of the Nathanson liver retractor. The retractor, with its multiple angled metal structure, is positioned skillfully to elevate the left hemiliver for optimal visualization of the stomach and surrounding structures.

The surgeon uses the Nathanson liver retractor to maneuver the liver into the desired position, ensuring equal pressure is applied over the inferior surface to achieve adequate retraction without causing liver ischemia.

The liver is retracted superiorly using the Nathanson liver retractor, providing a clear view of the operative area, including the lesser omentum, stomach, and greater omentum. The retractor is secured in place with a table fixation device outside the patient.

The surgeon begins peritoneal dissection, dividing the short gastric vessels and mobilizing the esophagus. The assistant uses a laparoscopic Babcock retractor to hold the fundus of the stomach to the patient's left side, while the primary surgeon lifts the lesser omentum, revealing a bloodless window that provides a clear plane for dissection towards the diaphragm's crura.

The surgeon divides the peritoneum overlying the anterior border of the esophagus. The right crus of the diaphragm becomes visible on the right-hand side through the window created by the division of the lesser omentum.

The surgeon carefully divides the peritoneum overlying the oesophagus, taking care to avoid damage to the anterior division of the vagus nerve. Following this, the fundus of the stomach is mobilized, preparing for the next stage of the procedure.

The surgeon divides the greater omentum from the greater curvature of the stomach, including the division of the short gastric vessels. The stomach is retracted to the patient's right, while the assistant uses a Babcock retractor to pull the greater omentum to the left.

The surgeon uses diathermy with a long, slow burn to carefully divide the short gastric vessels, which are visibly thick and could bleed briskly if not handled properly. The dissection is performed close to the stomach to minimize the risk of unnecessary hemorrhage.

The surgeon divides one of the short gastric vessels directly on the stomach, achieving maximum clearance of the surrounding omentum and fat. The remnant vessels appear large, highlighting the importance of careful dissection to facilitate manipulation of the stomach for the fundoplication wrap.

The surgeon performs blunt dissection in a cephalocordial direction to develop the plane between the left cruce of the diaphragm posteriorly and the oesophagus anteriorly, following the division of the greater omentum and short gastric vessels.

The surgeon develops a plane between the left cruce and the oesophagus, creating a posterior window. The oesophagus is positioned anteriorly, while the fundus of the stomach is pulled behind the oesophagus to form part of the Nissen 360-degree wrap.

The surgeon uses blunt dissection techniques, occasionally inserting tonsil swabs as tools to develop the plane between the oesophagus and the left cruce, enhancing the separation of these anatomical structures.

In this video of a different patient, the surgeon completes minimal blunt dissection on the right side, revealing an opening behind the oesophagus. A Johan instrument is passed through the window to confirm free passage, which will later allow the fundus of the stomach to be passed behind the oesophagus to create the 360-degree wrap.

The surgeon divides the final strands of peritoneum overlying the anterior aspect of the oesophagus, ensuring the oesophagus is mobile between five and ten centimeters below the diaphragmatic hiatus for the completion of a proper posterior wrap.

The surgeon uses a tonsil swab as a blunt dissector to develop the plane around the oesophagus, both posteriorly and anteriorly, while carefully avoiding the nearby heart and lungs to prevent entering the pleural cavity.

The surgeon develops the posterior window behind the oesophagus, carefully identifying and pointing to the posterior division of the vagus nerve to avoid damage. The nerve is visible and must be moved anteriorly with the oesophagus during the procedure.

The surgeon passes a Jake's catheter through the posterior window and secures it anteriorly using a Babcock retractor. This maneuver allows the assistant to move the top of the stomach and the oesophagus left and right, facilitating better access for further dissection of the posterior window.

The surgical field shows the liver, oesophagus, right crus, left crus, hiatus, and the posterior window, indicating that the oesophagus and crural dissection has been completed and the area is prepared for crural repair.

A schematic representation illustrates the principle of crural repair, where the surgeon sutures the left and right crus together to reduce the hiatus and prevent a hiatus hernia. This laparoscopic technique typically involves placing one to four interrupted sutures.

The surgeon performs the first suture of the crural repair, with the assistant retracting the oesophagus laterally to provide a clear view of the crura. The needle is passed through the left and right crus in a single pass, and the knot is tied according to the surgeon's preference.

In terms of how high up to go on the hiatal repair, the creel repair, this is very much dependent on how big the defect is from the outset. But it's also important to take into account this shouldn't be too tight in order to prevent the complication or side effect of dysphagia.

The anesthetist passes a bougie down the esophagus under direct vision to ensure it does not perforate the esophagus or stomach. This maneuver slightly increases the size of the esophagus, simulating the passage of a food bolus, allowing the surgeon to assess the snugness of the crural repair and decide if additional sutures are needed.
The surgeon ties the final suture of the crural repair, visible at the top of the screen, completing the repair with three sutures.

The surgeon begins the final stage of the fundoplication procedure, performing a 360-degree wrap of the stomach around the lower esophagus. This involves carefully positioning the gastric fundus to encircle the esophagus, creating a complete wrap to prevent reflux.

The surgeon identifies the fundus of the stomach and checks its mobility for wrapping. Using a Johan grasper, they pass through the posterior window behind the oesophagus, grasp the fundus, and pull it through. The shoeshine maneuver is performed to confirm adequate mobility of the stomach behind the oesophagus.

The surgeon uses an instrument to grasp the fundus of the stomach and passes it behind the dissected posterior window. The shoeshine maneuver is performed, moving the stomach back and forth behind the oesophagus, bringing the anterior aspect of the oesophagus and stomach together. The next step involves suturing the fundus back on itself over the oesophagus.

The surgeon places the first suture by grasping the stomach and pulling it over, suturing through the fundus. The assistant pulls down on the Jake's catheter while the primary surgeon maintains tension on the suture to bring both aspects of the stomach together.

The surgeon expertly places the final suture, securing the stomach in a 360-degree wrap around the esophagus. The suture is followed by a throw in the opposite direction, ensuring stability. The third and final suture is applied, completing the wrap with three sutures, as the stomach is already opposed.

It's thought to anchor the wrap onto the anterior part of the oesophagus but there is little evidence to suggest whether one technique is beneficial over the other one. Obviously by taking an anterior aspect of the oesophagus there is the risk of damaging the oesophagus. which is absent if this approach is not taken.
The final view of the operation shows the completed 360-degree fundal wrap around the back of the oesophagus, anchored anteriorly with three interrupted sutures.

As mentioned at the outset of this screencast, in the learning objectives, I will briefly mention the alternative procedures which are sometimes performed much less frequently than the NISN, as it has the most robust body of evidence for its long-term efficacy. However, the alternatives, one is a Dawes wrap, which is anterior, this is where the fundus is brought anterior over the esophagus, therefore you do not need to make the posterior window and then sutured onto the stomach or esophagus anteriorly. The toupee is the same principle as a NISN-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M-M
For various reasons, the Nissen has been adopted as the most common approach and is certainly the first line when combining the operation with a hiatus hernia repair.

The surgeon operates near the spleen while dividing the short gastric vessels, highlighting the proximity and potential for bleeding. The video shows careful dissection around the stomach and esophagus to avoid visceral perforation. During the hiatal dissection, attention is given to avoid entering the pleural cavity, which could lead to pneumothoraces.

In terms of post-operative complications, one of the most frequently reported is gas bloat. This is where patients are unable to pass gas by the oral route and therefore they feel an element of discomfort, like their stomach has become bloated. Equally, depending on the technique used, if the wrap is too tight or the accrual repair is too tight, patients can suffer with dysphagia. Again, in relation to the gas bloat, as gas is not travelling upwards, it travels downwards, and so some patients complain of troublesome flatulence. And finally, probably the most common over the long term, certainly after ten years, a significant proportion of patients, and when I say significant, maybe up towards ten, twenty percent, will report recurrent symptoms.

And this is most frequently identified by asking them whether they're back on their anti-acid medications, such as a H-II antagonist or a proton pump inhibitor. So going back to our learning outcomes, hopefully by the end of this screencast, you will now have knowledge of the relevant surgical anatomy. You'll be aware of the key operative steps involved in a Nissen, three hundred and sixty degree fundoplication. Briefly, an overview of the alternative laparoscopic antireflux procedures and recall the important complications of antireflux surgery.

So thank you very much for joining us for this new podcast, Anti-Reflux Surgery. Remember you can follow us on Facebook by searching School of Surgery, on iTunes or via schoolofsurgery.podomatic.com. I'd like to thank Mr. Sean Appleton who performed the surgery for this podcast and he was assisted by both myself and surgical care practitioner Glynis Howard. Thank you very much.
